proof_trace: Release callback adev_release() calls auxiliary_device_uninit() which by kernel convention calls put_device(), potentially freeing the adev struct container. Subsequent access to adev->name (L193) and adev (L194) becomes use-after-free.
attack_scenario: Device cleanup triggers adev_release() callback, auxiliary_device_uninit() frees the container struct, then kfree(adev->name) and kfree(adev) access freed memory causing kernel corruption or crash.

proof_trace: After device_unregister() potentially frees the device (when refcount reaches zero), device->deleted = true accesses freed memory. No additional references are held to prevent immediate deallocation.
attack_scenario: User writes '1' to /sys/bus/peci/devices/<device>/remove when no drivers are bound, triggering immediate UAF in peci_device_destroy().
reason: UAF — device_unregister frees then device->deleted accessed

proof_trace: Race condition between device removal and I/O operations leads to UAF. aspeed_peci_check_idle() accesses priv->rst without synchronization while device removal can trigger devres cleanup and free the reset control. The device_unregister() call in unregister_controller() does not wait for pending aspeed_peci_xfer() operations.
attack_scenario: Attacker triggers PECI I/O operation while simultaneously causing device removal (e.g., module unload), causing reset_control_assert() to dereference freed memory in aspeed_peci_check_idle().
reason: Race UAF — aspeed_peci_check_idle vs devres cleanup
